Prochaine action : choisir entre sous-agent swift-reviewer, REVIEW-LESSONS.md auto, ou extensions docs/SWIFT-*.md. (disable recaps in /config)

message.content: il n'existe pas de linter swift qui permettrait d'implémenter tout ou partie des checks sans avoir à introduire un script sh dedié ?

content.text:
Oui — **SwiftLint** est le standard de facto et couvrirait 6 des 7 checks avec de bien meilleures garanties qu'un script shell.

## Mapping checks → SwiftLint

| Code | SwiftLint |
|---|---|
| E1 `try?` sur FS critiques | `custom_rules` regex |
| E2 `flock(LOCK_EX)` sans `LOCK_NB` | `custom_rules` regex + `excluded: Tests/` |
| W1 `nonisolated(unsafe)` | `custom_rules` regex + `match_kinds: [identifier, keyword]` (ignore automatiquement les commentaires, contrairement à mon script) |
| W2 Formatter alloué hors déclaration | `custom_rules` regex avec `match_kinds: [identifier]` |
| W3 `Task.sleep` dans Tests | `custom_rules` avec `included: .*Tests.*` |
| W4 `@unchecked Sendable` | `custom_rules` regex |
| W5 Drift commentaire durée | `custom_rules` + `match_kinds: [comment]` |

Plus une **built-in `force_try`** (= notre pattern `try!` dans contexte `async throws`) qu'on n'avait pas su détecter proprement en shell.

## Avantages concrets

1. **`match_kinds`** : distingue commentaire / string / identifier via SourceKit — supprime tous les faux positifs qui ont nécessité mes garde-fous `^[[:space:]]*//` en bash.
2. **Baseline** (`swiftlint --baseline`) : fige les 13 warnings existants, ne fait échouer que sur les **régressions**. Résout exactement le problème que je contourne avec "les WARN s'affichent seulement si gitdiff-watcher voit le fichier modifié".
3. **Output standard** : `file:line:col: severity: message (rule_id)` — parseable, cliquable dans Xcode/IDE.
4. **Severity par règle** : `severity: error` ou `warning` — on garde la distinction E/W.
5. **Intégration Xcode** : warnings inline sans aucune action, meilleure visibilité pour les non-Claude qui ouvrent le projet.

## Coût

- Dépendance : `brew install swiftlint` ou SwiftPM plugin (`.package(url: "nicklockwood/SwiftFormat")` / `realm/SwiftLint`).
- `.swiftlint.yml` à la racine du package (~40 lignes pour nos 7 règles + baseline).
- Temps d'exécution : ~1-3 s sur ce repo (vs. <100 ms du script shell), acceptable dans le hook Stop.
- Remplacement : on appelle `swiftlint lint --quiet --strict` au lieu du script bash, avec `gitdiff-watcher` qui passe les fichiers modifiés.

## Limite unique

Il n'y a pas d'équivalent direct pour détecter « Formatter alloué hors déclaration » **sans regex négative** — SwiftLint `match_kinds` aide mais ne connaît pas le contexte enclosing (function body vs. property). Le regex de mon script contourne ça pareil, donc pas pire.

---

Veux-tu que je migre ? Je propose :
1. Ajouter SwiftLint comme SwiftPM plugin (pas brew, pour éviter un préalable machine) 
2. Écrire `.swiftlint.yml` avec nos 7 custom_rules + baseline du code actuel
3. Remplacer le hook bash par `swiftlint lint --quiet` sur le diff
4. Supprimer `check-swift-quality.sh` (ou le garder le temps d'une PR de transition)

Si tu préfères garder le shell pour l'instant, on peut aussi **coexister** : SwiftLint pour le bulk, script bash pour la règle W5 (drift commentaire) qui est notre finding le plus spécifique et qui ne mérite pas forcément une adoption SwiftLint complète.

content.content:
To do this, add the plugin to the target(s) to be linted as follows:

```swift
.target(
    ...
    plugins: [.plugin(name: "SwiftLintBuildToolPlugin", package: "SwiftLintPlugins")]
),
```

### Swift Package Command Plugin

> [!NOTE]
> Requires installing via [Swift Package Manager](#swift-package-manager).

The command plugin enables running SwiftLint from the command line as follows:

```shell
swift package plugin swiftlint
```

### Xcode Projects

> [!NOTE]
> Requires installing via [Xcode Package Dependency](#xcode-package-dependency).

Build tool plugins run as a build phase of each target. When a project has
multiple targets, the plugin must be added to the desired targets individually.

To do this, add the `SwiftLintBuildToolPlugin` to the `Run Build Tool Plug-ins`
phase of the `Build Phases` for the target(s) to be linted.

> [!TIP]
> When using the plugin for the first time, be sure to trust and enable
> it when prompted. If a macros build warning exists, select it to trust
> and enable the macros as well.

For unattended use (e.g. on CI), package plugin and macro
validations can be disabled with either of the following:

* Using `xcodebuild` options:

  ```bash
  -skipPackagePluginValidation
  -skipMacroValidation
  ```

* Setting Xcode defaults:

  ```bash
  defaults write com.apple.dt.Xcode IDESkipPackagePluginFingerprintValidatation -bool YES
  defaults write com.apple.dt.Xcode IDESkipMacroFingerprintValidation -bool YES
  ```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The unattended use options bypass Xcode's validation dialogs
> and implicitly trust all plugins and macros, which has security implications.

#### Unexpected Xcode Project Structures

Project structures where SwiftLint's configuration file is located
outside of the package/project directory are not directly supported
by the build tool plugin. This is because it isn't possible to pass
arguments to build tool plugins (e.g., passing the config file path).

If your project structure doesn't work directly with the build tool
plugin, please consider one of the following options:

* To use a config file located outside the package/project directory, a config
  file may be added to that directory specifying a parent config path to the
  other config file, e.g., `parent_config: path/to/.swiftlint.yml`.
* You can also consider the use of a
  [Run Script Build Phase](#xcode-run-script-build-phase) in place of the build
  tool plugin.

### Xcode Run Script Build Phase

> [!NOTE]
> Based upon the installation method used, the shell command syntax in the
> Run Script Build Phase may be different or additional configuration could
> be required. Refer to the [installation](#installation) instructions for
> more information.

If the build tool plugin does not work for your project setup or when
additional custom setup is required, SwiftLint can be added as a Run Script
Build Phase. This is useful when a project setup relies on the `--config`
SwiftLint option; or to lint all targets together in a single `swiftlint`
invocation. File inclusions and exclusions can be configured in the
[`.swiftlint.yml` configuration](#configuration).

To do this, add a custom script to a `Run Script` phase of the `Build Phases`
of the primary app target, after the `Compile Sources` phase. Use the
following script implementation:

```bash
if command -v swiftlint >/dev/null 2>&1
then
    swiftlint
else
    echo "warning: `swiftlint` command not found - See https://github.com/realm/SwiftLint#installation for installation instructions."

phase of the `Build Phases` for the target(s) to be linted.

> [!TIP]
> When using the plugin for the first time, be sure to trust and enable
> it when prompted. If a macros build warning exists, select it to trust
> and enable the macros as well.

For unattended use (e.g. on CI), package plugin and macro
validations can be disabled with either of the following:

* Using `xcodebuild` options:

  ```bash
  -skipPackagePluginValidation
  -skipMacroValidation
  ```

* Setting Xcode defaults:

  ```bash
  defaults write com.apple.dt.Xcode IDESkipPackagePluginFingerprintValidatation -bool YES
  defaults write com.apple.dt.Xcode IDESkipMacroFingerprintValidation -bool YES
  ```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The unattended use options bypass Xcode's validation dialogs
> and implicitly trust all plugins and macros, which has security implications.

#### Unexpected Xcode Project Structures

Project structures where SwiftLint's configuration file is located
outside of the package/project directory are not directly supported
by the build tool plugin. This is because it isn't possible to pass
arguments to build tool plugins (e.g., passing the config file path).

If your project structure doesn't work directly with the build tool
plugin, please consider one of the following options:

* To use a config file located outside the package/project directory, a config
  file may be added to that directory specifying a parent config path to the
  other config file, e.g., `parent_config: path/to/.swiftlint.yml`.
* You can also consider the use of a
  [Run Script Build Phase](#xcode-run-script-build-phase) in place of the build
  tool plugin.

### Xcode Run Script Build Phase

> [!NOTE]
> Based upon the installation method used, the shell command syntax in the
> Run Script Build Phase may be different or additional configuration could
> be required. Refer to the [installation](#installation) instructions for
> more information.

If the build tool plugin does not work for your project setup or when
additional custom setup is required, SwiftLint can be added as a Run Script
Build Phase. This is useful when a project setup relies on the `--config`
SwiftLint option; or to lint all targets together in a single `swiftlint`
invocation. File inclusions and exclusions can be configured in the
[`.swiftlint.yml` configuration](#configuration).

To do this, add a custom script to a `Run Script` phase of the `Build Phases`
of the primary app target, after the `Compile Sources` phase. Use the
following script implementation:

```bash
if command -v swiftlint >/dev/null 2>&1
then
    swiftlint
else
    echo "warning: `swiftlint` command not found - See https://github.com/realm/SwiftLint#installation for installation instructions."

J'ai la syntaxe confirmée. Migration planifiée :

1. Ajouter `SwiftLintPlugins` (repo recommandé par SwiftLint) au `Package.swift` comme **build tool plugin** → le lint tourne à chaque `swift build` existant, pas de hook dédié à ajouter.
2. Créer `.swiftlint.yml` avec 6 `custom_rules` (on laisse tomber W2 qui demande un lookbehind non supporté).
3. Générer une `.swiftlint.baseline` pour masquer les 13 warnings legacy.
4. Supprimer `check-swift-quality.sh` et son hook.

# SwiftLint configuration for AIUsagesTrackers.
#
# Rule codes mirror .claude/rules/swift-quality.md — keep the mapping in sync.
# Severities: `error` fails the build; `warning` surfaces in logs / IDE.

included:
  - Sources
  - Tests

# Turn off the broad built-in catalogue; we opt in explicitly to keep noise low.
disabled_rules:
  - trailing_whitespace
  - line_length
  - file_length
  - type_body_length
  - function_body_length
  - identifier_name
  - todo

opt_in_rules:
  - force_unwrapping  # surfaces runtime-input `!` dereferences (SWIFT-TESTABILITY)

# Built-in rule tuning.
force_try:
  severity: warning  # test helpers still use try! legitimately; audit case-by-case.

custom_rules:
  e1_try_silencing_critical_fs:
    name: "try? on critical filesystem op"
    regex: 'try\?\s+(FileManager\.default\.)?(createDirectory|moveItem|copyItem|setAttributes)\b'
    message: "try? swallows failure on a correctness-critical FS op — use do/catch (SWIFT-ERROR-HANDLING §1)"
    severity: error

  e2_flock_lock_ex_without_nb:
    name: "flock LOCK_EX without LOCK_NB"
    regex: 'flock\([^)]*LOCK_EX(?![^)]*LOCK_NB)'
    message: "flock with LOCK_EX must be OR'd with LOCK_NB and wrapped in a timeout loop (SWIFT-CONCURRENCY / SWIFT-IO-ROBUSTNESS)"
    severity: error
    excluded:
      - "AIUsagesTrackers/Tests/.*\\.swift"

  w1_nonisolated_unsafe:
    name: "nonisolated(unsafe) in production"
    regex: 'nonisolated\(unsafe\)'
    match_kinds:
      - keyword
      - identifier
    message: "nonisolated(unsafe) bypasses Swift isolation — prefer actor-isolated storage or per-call allocation (SWIFT-CONCURRENCY)"
    severity: warning
    excluded:
      - "AIUsagesTrackers/Tests/.*\\.swift"

  w3_task_sleep_literal_in_tests:
    name: "Task.sleep with fixed literal in tests"
    regex: 'Task\.sleep\([^)]*[0-9]'
    message: "Task.sleep with a fixed literal is fragile under CI load — poll the observable state with the eventually() helper (SWIFT-TESTABILITY)"
    severity: warning
    included:
      - "AIUsagesTrackers/Tests/.*\\.swift"

  w4_unchecked_sendable:
    name: "@unchecked Sendable"
    regex: '@unchecked\s+Sendable'
    match_kinds:
      - attribute.builtin
      - identifier
      - keyword
    message: "@unchecked Sendable requires an adjacent justification comment describing the single-threaded contract (SWIFT-CONCURRENCY)"
    severity: warning

  w5_duration_literal_in_comment:
    name: "Duration literal embedded in comment"
    regex: '\b\d+\s*(hours?|days?|minutes?|seconds?|ms)\b'
    match_kinds:
      - comment
    message: "Comment embeds a bare `N unit` duration — lift to a named constant whose declaration site carries the rationale (SWIFT-TESTABILITY §Comment WHY)"
    severity: warning

toolUseResult.newString:
included:
  - Sources
  - Tests

# Narrow the SwiftLint surface to exactly the codes documented in
# .claude/rules/swift-quality.md. Built-in rules are deliberately NOT enabled —
# we want a low-noise, high-signal lint focused on the patterns that reviews
# keep flagging. Reach for built-in rules only if a review finding recurs that
# a custom rule cannot express precisely.

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# SwiftLint configuration for AIUsagesTrackers.
#
# Rule codes mirror .claude/rules/swift-quality.md — keep the mapping in sync.
# Severities: `error` fails the build; `warning` surfaces in logs / IDE.

included:
  - Sources
  - Tests

# Turn off the broad built-in catalogue; we opt in explicitly to keep noise low.
disabled_rules:
  - trailing_whitespace
  - line_length
  - file_length
  - type_body_length
  - function_body_length
  - identifier_name
  - todo

opt_in_rules:
  - force_unwrapping  # surfaces runtime-input `!` dereferences (SWIFT-TESTABILITY)

# Built-in rule tuning.
force_try:
  severity: warning  # test helpers still use try! legitimately; audit case-by-case.

custom_rules:
  e1_try_silencing_critical_fs:
    name: "try? on critical filesystem op"
    regex: 'try\?\s+(FileManager\.default\.)?(createDirectory|moveItem|copyItem|setAttributes)\b'
    message: "try? swallows failure on a correctness-critical FS op — use do/catch (SWIFT-ERROR-HANDLING §1)"
    severity: error

  e2_flock_lock_ex_without_nb:
    name: "flock LOCK_EX without LOCK_NB"
    regex: 'flock\([^)]*LOCK_EX(?![^)]*LOCK_NB)'
    message: "flock with LOCK_EX must be OR'd with LOCK_NB and wrapped in a timeout loop (SWIFT-CONCURRENCY / SWIFT-IO-ROBUSTNESS)"
    severity: error
    excluded:
      - "AIUsagesTrackers/Tests/.*\\.swift"

  w1_nonisolated_unsafe:
    name: "nonisolated(unsafe) in production"
    regex: 'nonisolated\(unsafe\)'
    match_kinds:
      - keyword
      - identifier
    message: "nonisolated(unsafe) bypasses Swift isolation — prefer actor-isolated storage or per-call allocation (SWIFT-CONCURRENCY)"
    severity: warning
    excluded:
      - "AIUsagesTrackers/Tests/.*\\.swift"

  w3_task_sleep_literal_in_tests:
    name: "Task.sleep with fixed literal in tests"
    regex: 'Task\.sleep\([^)]*[0-9]'
    message: "Task.sleep with a fixed literal is fragile under CI load — poll the observable state with the eventually() helper (SWIFT-TESTABILITY)"
    severity: warning
    included:
      - "AIUsagesTrackers/Tests/.*\\.swift"

  w4_unchecked_sendable:
    name: "@unchecked Sendable"
    regex: '@unchecked\s+Sendable'
    match_kinds:
      - attribute.builtin
      - identifier
      - keyword
    message: "@unchecked Sendable requires an adjacent justification comment describing the single-threaded contract (SWIFT-CONCURRENCY)"
    severity: warning

  w5_duration_literal_in_comment:
    name: "Duration literal embedded in comment"
    regex: '\b\d+\s*(hours?|days?|minutes?|seconds?|ms)\b'
    match_kinds:
      - comment
    message: "Comment embeds a bare `N unit` duration — lift to a named constant whose declaration site carries the rationale (SWIFT-TESTABILITY §Comment WHY)"
    severity: warning

lines: -# Turn off the broad built-in catalogue; we opt in explicitly to keep noise low.
+# Narrow the SwiftLint surface to exactly the codes documented in
+# .claude/rules/swift-quality.md. Built-in rules are deliberately NOT enabled —
+# we want a low-noise, high-signal lint focused on the patterns that reviews
+# keep flagging. Reach for built-in rules only if a review finding recurs that
+# a custom rule cannot express precisely.
-  severity: warning  # test helpers still use try! legitimately; audit case-by-case.

# SwiftLint configuration for AIUsagesTrackers.
#
# Rule codes mirror .claude/rules/swift-quality.md — keep the mapping in sync.
# Severities: `error` fails the build; `warning` surfaces in logs / IDE.

included:
  - Sources
  - Tests

# Narrow the SwiftLint surface to exactly the codes documented in
# .claude/rules/swift-quality.md. Built-in rules are deliberately NOT enabled —
# we want a low-noise, high-signal lint focused on the patterns that reviews
# keep flagging. Reach for built-in rules only if a review finding recurs that
# a custom rule cannot express precisely.
only_rules:
  - custom_rules

custom_rules:
  e1_try_silencing_critical_fs:
    name: "try? on critical filesystem op"
    regex: 'try\?\s+(FileManager\.default\.)?(createDirectory|moveItem|copyItem|setAttributes)\b'
    message: "try? swallows failure on a correctness-critical FS op — use do/catch (SWIFT-ERROR-HANDLING §1)"
    severity: error

  e2_flock_lock_ex_without_nb:
    name: "flock LOCK_EX without LOCK_NB"
    regex: 'flock\([^)]*LOCK_EX(?![^)]*LOCK_NB)'
    message: "flock with LOCK_EX must be OR'd with LOCK_NB and wrapped in a timeout loop (SWIFT-CONCURRENCY / SWIFT-IO-ROBUSTNESS)"
    severity: error
    excluded:
      - "AIUsagesTrackers/Tests/.*\\.swift"

  w1_nonisolated_unsafe:
    name: "nonisolated(unsafe) in production"
    regex: 'nonisolated\(unsafe\)'
    match_kinds:
      - keyword
      - identifier
    message: "nonisolated(unsafe) bypasses Swift isolation — prefer actor-isolated storage or per-call allocation (SWIFT-CONCURRENCY)"
    severity: warning
    excluded:
      - "AIUsagesTrackers/Tests/.*\\.swift"

  w3_task_sleep_literal_in_tests:
    name: "Task.sleep with fixed literal in tests"
    regex: 'Task\.sleep\([^)]*[0-9]'
    message: "Task.sleep with a fixed literal is fragile under CI load — poll the observable state with the eventually() helper (SWIFT-TESTABILITY)"
    severity: warning
    included:
      - "AIUsagesTrackers/Tests/.*\\.swift"

  w4_unchecked_sendable:
    name: "@unchecked Sendable"
    regex: '@unchecked\s+Sendable'
    match_kinds:
      - attribute.builtin
      - identifier
      - keyword
    message: "@unchecked Sendable requires an adjacent justification comment describing the single-threaded contract (SWIFT-CONCURRENCY)"
    severity: warning

  w5_duration_literal_in_comment:
    name: "Duration literal embedded in comment"
    regex: '\b\d+\s*(hours?|days?|minutes?|seconds?|ms)\b'
    match_kinds:
      - comment
    message: "Comment embeds a bare `N unit` duration — lift to a named constant whose declaration site carries the rationale (SWIFT-TESTABILITY §Comment WHY)"
    severity: warning

      - comment
    message: "Comment embeds a bare `N unit` duration — lift to a named constant whose declaration site carries the rationale (SWIFT-TESTABILITY §Comment WHY)"

---
title: Resolve SwiftLint legacy warnings captured in .swiftlint.baseline
date: 2026-04-18
criticality: medium
size: S
---

## Problem

`AIUsagesTrackers/.swiftlint.baseline` lists 16 violations that existed when
the six custom rules (E1, E2, W1, W3, W4, W5) were introduced. Each
violation is a real finding already surfaced in prior review passes; the
baseline only silences them so the build stays green. As long as an entry
sits in the baseline, the rule is passively disabled for that site — a new
agent modifying the same region will not learn from the finding.

Breakdown:

- **W1 — `nonisolated(unsafe)` in production** (×1)
  - `Sources/AIUsagesTrackers/Logging/Logger.swift:31` — static ISO formatter.
- **W3 — `Task.sleep` literal in tests** (×8)
  - `Tests/AIUsagesTrackersTests/UsageStoreTests.swift:80, 369, 747`
  - `Tests/AIUsagesTrackersTests/UsagePollerTests.swift:143, 236`
  - `Tests/AIUsagesTrackersTests/UsagesFileWatcherTests.swift:33, 88, 114`
- **W4 — `@unchecked Sendable` without a justification comment** (×2)
  - `Tests/AIUsagesTrackersTests/ClaudeCodeConnectorTests.swift:7`
  - `Tests/AIUsagesTrackersTests/UsageStoreTests.swift:11`
- **W5 — duration literal embedded in a comment** (×5)
  - `Tests/AIUsagesTrackersTests/UsageStoreTests.swift:438 (×3), 510, 543`

## Impact

- **Maintainability**: baseline entries are a hidden rule carve-out; the
  longer they persist, the less the harness teaches new work.
- **AI code generation quality**: agents copying from these sites replicate
  the exact patterns the rules were introduced to prevent.
- **Bug/regression risk**: W1 (single thread formatter) and W3 (fragile
  async sync) are the real correctness/flake risks; W4 and W5 are code
  quality.

## Affected files / areas

- `AIUsagesTrackers/.swiftlint.baseline` — remove entries as each site is
  fixed.
- The 8 files listed above.

## Refactoring paths

1. **W1 in `Logger.swift`**: move `isoFormatter` off a `nonisolated(unsafe)
   static let` to a dedicated serial `DispatchQueue` or instantiate per
   call — `Logger` is not in a hot path, per-call allocation is acceptable.
2. **W3 in tests**: introduce an `eventually(timeout:interval:_:)` helper
   (already exists in `UsageStoreTests` — promote to a shared test utility)
   and replace every fixed `Task.sleep` that waits for an observable
   state change. Pure time-based waits (no observable state to poll, e.g.
   "no emit happens within N ms") can stay with a `Task.sleep` behind a
   named constant + rationale comment.
3. **W4**: add a one-line justification comment directly above each
   `@unchecked Sendable` conformance explaining why single-threaded access
   is safe.
4. **W5**: lift every `// N <unit>` comment to a named constant whose
   declaration site carries the rationale. Update the test body to reference
   the constant.
5. After each batch, regenerate `.swiftlint.baseline`:
   `swift package plugin --allow-writing-to-package-directory swiftlint lint --write-baseline .swiftlint.baseline`
   and commit the shrunken baseline alongside the fix.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] `.swiftlint.baseline` is empty or the file is removed along with the
  `baseline:` key in `.swiftlint.yml`.
- [ ] `swift build` passes with zero SwiftLint warnings.
- [ ] No new `Task.sleep(fixed literal)` remains on a site where an
  observable state could be polled instead.
- [ ] Every surviving `@unchecked Sendable` has a justification comment on
  the preceding line.

## Additional context

Introduced alongside the SwiftLint migration (see commit that adds
`AIUsagesTrackers/.swiftlint.yml`). The rules themselves correspond to
recurring findings from reviews on PRs #3–#5; see `.claude/rules/swift-quality.md`
for the rule catalog and `docs/SWIFT-*.md` for the underlying specs.

input.old_string:
## Automated check on Stop

`.claude/scripts/check-swift-quality.sh` runs via `gitdiff-watcher` on every modified `*.swift` file. Findings use the codes below; resolve errors before committing and avoid reintroducing warnings.

| Code | Severity | Pattern | Rule source |
|------|----------|---------|-------------|
| E1   | ERROR    | `try?` on `createDirectory` / `moveItem` / `copyItem` / `setAttributes` | SWIFT-ERROR-HANDLING §1 |
| E2   | ERROR    | `flock(..., LOCK_EX)` in production code without `LOCK_NB` | SWIFT-CONCURRENCY + SWIFT-IO-ROBUSTNESS |
| W1   | WARN     | `nonisolated(unsafe)` in production sources | SWIFT-CONCURRENCY |
| W2   | WARN     | `ISO8601DateFormatter()` / `DateFormatter()` / `NumberFormatter()` allocated inside a function body | SWIFT-TESTABILITY §Reuse expensive objects |
| W3   | WARN     | `Task.sleep(literal)` in tests — prefer an `eventually()` poll-helper | SWIFT-TESTABILITY |
| W4   | WARN     | `@unchecked Sendable` without an adjacent justification comment | SWIFT-CONCURRENCY |
| W5   | WARN     | Comment embeds a bare `N unit` duration — inline drift risk after constant extraction | SWIFT-TESTABILITY §Comment WHY |

When a finding fires on a file that you did not author, fix it under the same change rather than leaving drift behind.
input.new_string:
## Automated check via SwiftLint

`AIUsagesTrackers/.swiftlint.yml` declares six custom rules enforced by
SwiftLint, wired as a SwiftPM build tool plugin on every target. The rules
run on every `swift build` (and inside Xcode). Findings surface with these
codes — resolve errors before committing and avoid reintroducing warnings.

| Code | Severity | Pattern | Rule source |
|------|----------|---------|-------------|
| E1   | error    | `try?` on `createDirectory` / `moveItem` / `copyItem` / `setAttributes` | SWIFT-ERROR-HANDLING §1 |
| E2   | error    | `flock(..., LOCK_EX)` in production code without `LOCK_NB` | SWIFT-CONCURRENCY + SWIFT-IO-ROBUSTNESS |
| W1   | warning  | `nonisolated(unsafe)` in production sources | SWIFT-CONCURRENCY |
| W3   | warning  | `Task.sleep(literal)` in tests — prefer an `eventually()` poll-helper | SWIFT-TESTABILITY |
| W4   | warning  | `@unchecked Sendable` without an adjacent justification comment | SWIFT-CONCURRENCY |
| W5   | warning  | Comment embeds a bare `N unit` duration — drift risk after constant extraction | SWIFT-TESTABILITY §Comment WHY |

Violations already present when the rules were introduced are recorded in
`AIUsagesTrackers/.swiftlint.baseline` and do not fail the build; new
violations matching the same rules do. When a finding fires on a file you
did not author, fix it under the same change rather than leaving drift
behind, and regenerate the baseline with:

```
cd AIUsagesTrackers && swift package plugin --allow-writing-to-package-directory swiftlint lint --write-baseline .swiftlint.baseline
```

toolUseResult.originalFile:
---
description: Swift production quality rules — load when writing or modifying Swift source files
globs: ["**/*.swift", "**/Package.swift"]
---

# Swift production quality

Before writing or modifying any Swift code, load and follow these docs:

- `docs/SWIFT-CONCURRENCY.md` — cooperative thread pool, NSFormatter thread safety, actors, Process timeouts
- `docs/SWIFT-ERROR-HANDLING.md` — no silent `try?`, no success-after-catch, rich error types
- `docs/SWIFT-IO-ROBUSTNESS.md` — atomic writes, flock with timeout, O(n+m) merges
- `docs/SWIFT-TESTABILITY.md` — dependency injection, test coverage, force-unwrap, comments, magic numbers
- `docs/SWIFT-VALUE-OBJECTS.md` — value objects for domain fields, struct vs enum, ExpressibleByXxx, Codable

## Non-negotiable rules

1. **Never block the cooperative thread pool** — no `Process.waitUntilExit()`, `readDataToEndOfFile()`, or `queue.sync` from an `async`/`actor` context without a background dispatch.

2. **Never use `try?` on operations whose failure affects correctness** — `createDirectory`, `write`, `flock`, file moves: propagate or handle explicitly with a log.

3. **Every external dependency must be injectable via a protocol** — `URLSession`, Keychain, `FileManager`, `Process`, clock. No direct calls to system APIs in business logic.

4. **Every public method must have at least one test** — especially network, I/O, lifecycle (start/stop idempotence), and all error paths.

5. **Always write files atomically** — use `Data.write(to:options: .atomic)` or write to a temp file then `replaceItemAt(_:withItemAt:)`. Never `removeItem` + `moveItem`.

6. **Error enums must carry associated values** with diagnostic context (status code, underlying error, path, etc.).

7. **Comment WHY, not WHAT** — no restating code in plain English. Extract magic numbers as named constants.

8. **Wrap domain primitives in value objects** — never use raw `String` or `Int` for fields with a distinct identity (emails, vendor names, ISO dates, percentages, durations). See `docs/SWIFT-VALUE-OBJECTS.md` for the pattern.

## Automated check on Stop

`.claude/scripts/check-swift-quality.sh` runs via `gitdiff-watcher` on every modified `*.swift` file. Findings use the codes below; resolve errors before committing and avoid reintroducing warnings.

| Code | Severity | Pattern | Rule source |
|------|----------|---------|-------------|
| E1   | ERROR    | `try?` on `createDirectory` / `moveItem` / `copyItem` / `setAttributes` | SWIFT-ERROR-HANDLING §1 |
| E2   | ERROR    | `flock(..., LOCK_EX)` in production code without `LOCK_NB` | SWIFT-CONCURRENCY + SWIFT-IO-ROBUSTNESS |
| W1   | WARN     | `nonisolated(unsafe)` in production sources | SWIFT-CONCURRENCY |
| W2   | WARN     | `ISO8601DateFormatter()` / `DateFormatter()` / `NumberFormatter()` allocated inside a function body | SWIFT-TESTABILITY §Reuse expensive objects |
| W3   | WARN     | `Task.sleep(literal)` in tests — prefer an `eventually()` poll-helper | SWIFT-TESTABILITY |
| W4   | WARN     | `@unchecked Sendable` without an adjacent justification comment | SWIFT-CONCURRENCY |
| W5   | WARN     | Comment embeds a bare `N unit` duration — inline drift risk after constant extraction | SWIFT-TESTABILITY §Comment WHY |

When a finding fires on a file that you did not author, fix it under the same change rather than leaving drift behind.

content.text:
Migration SwiftLint complète. Récapitulatif final :

## Changements

**Ajouts**
- `AIUsagesTrackers/Package.swift` — dépendance `SwiftLintPlugins` attachée aux 3 targets (lib + app + tests) comme build tool plugin.
- `AIUsagesTrackers/.swiftlint.yml` — config avec `only_rules: [custom_rules]` + 6 règles E1/E2/W1/W3/W4/W5 + `baseline:` pointant vers `.swiftlint.baseline`.
- `AIUsagesTrackers/.swiftlint.baseline` — 16 violations legacy figées (1×W1, 8×W3, 2×W4, 5×W5).
- `tech-debt/REDACTED/debt.md` — plan de résolution des 16 entrées legacy, médium/S.

**Suppressions**
- `.claude/scripts/check-swift-quality.sh` (117 lignes bash → règles YAML précises).
- Hook `gitdiff-watcher` spécifique pour Swift quality dans `.claude/settings.json` (remplacé par l'intégration dans le `swift build` existant, dont le timeout est monté à 180 s pour absorber le premier cold build du plugin).

**Mises à jour**
- `.claude/rules/swift-quality.md` — tableau pointe sur SwiftLint, ajoute la commande de régénération de baseline. W2 retiré (pattern exigeait un lookbehind à largeur variable non supporté par `NSRegularExpression` ; il faudra un vrai AST-tool si on veut le couvrir).

## État validé

- `swift build` : 0 warning, 0 error (baseline absorbe le legacy).
- `swift package plugin swiftlint lint` : détecte bien les 16 patterns historiques ; le jour où un diff introduit un nouveau E1/E2, ça échoue le build immédiatement.

## Points à retenir pour la suite

- **W2** (formatter alloué hors déclaration) est perdu ; l'ajouter nécessite soit un AST-tool, soit un suivi via revue. Cas présents dans `ValueObjects.swift` à surveiller manuellement.
- La **baseline** doit rétrécir avec chaque PR qui touche un site legacy — ne pas la laisser pourrir, sinon les règles deviennent invisibles.
- Le **command plugin** (`swift package plugin swiftlint lint`) est dispo à la demande pour reviewer manuellement sans passer par un build complet.

# Smart commit

Tu dois committer les changements git en cours dans le repo courant en suivant les guidelines ci-dessous.

## Contexte utilisateur

Intent fourni par l'utilisateur (peut être vide) : ``

## Étapes

### 1. Analyser l'état du repo

Lance en parallèle :
- `git status` (sans `-uall`)
- `git diff --staged` et `git diff` pour voir les changements
- `git log -n 5 --pretty=format:'%h %s%n%b%n---'` pour voir les 4-5 derniers commits

### 2. Détecter les conventions du projet

À partir des 4-5 derniers commits :
- **Conventional commits ?** Si la majorité suit le format `type(scope): message` (feat, fix, chore, docs, refactor, test, perf, build, ci...) → utilise ce format. Sinon → format libre.
- **Langue ?** Détecte la langue dominante des messages (français, anglais, autre) → utilise la même langue. En cas de doute, français.
- **Style ?** Impératif vs descriptif, majuscule initiale ou non, présence de scope, etc. → imite.

### 3. Détecter un éventuel fixup

Si l'intent utilisateur mentionne un correctif (mots-clés : "fix", "corrige", "répare", "bug", "issue", "problème", "régression", "broken"...) :

1. Identifie les fichiers modifiés (`git diff --name-only HEAD` + fichiers staged).
2. Pour chaque fichier, regarde `git log -n 10 --oneline -- <fichier>` pour voir quels commits récents l'ont touché.
3. Si **un seul commit récent** (dans les ~5 derniers, non encore pushé sur main/master partagé — vérifie avec `git log @{u}..HEAD` si upstream) est clairement à l'origine du code cassé, propose un **`git commit --fixup=<sha>`** au lieu d'un commit classique.
4. Si ambigu ou si le commit date d'avant `@{u}` (déjà pushé en amont partagé), reste sur un commit classique.

Ne fais **pas** de rebase autosquash automatiquement — laisse ce soin à l'utilisateur.

### 4. Rédiger le message

Règles impératives :
- **Le message explique le POURQUOI**, pas le QUOI. Le diff dit déjà ce qui change. Le message dit pourquoi ce changement existe (motivation, contrainte, problème résolu, contexte métier).
- Sujet court (≤72 caractères), en suivant la convention détectée.
- Si le pourquoi ne tient pas dans le sujet, ajoute un corps (ligne vide + explication). Sinon, pas de corps inutile.
- Pas de name-dropping de fichiers/fonctions dans le sujet — le diff suffit.
- Pas de phrases vides genre « Mise à jour du code » ou « Divers fixes ».

Si l'intent utilisateur est vide ou trop vague pour déduire le pourquoi, et que le diff ne le rend pas évident, **demande à l'utilisateur** avant de committer plutôt que d'inventer.

### 5. Staging et commit

- Si rien n'est staged, stage les fichiers pertinents **par nom** (jamais `git add -A` ni `git add .` pour éviter d'embarquer secrets/binaires).
- Ignore les fichiers suspects (`.env`, `credentials*`, clés privées, dumps volumineux). Préviens l'utilisateur si tu en vois.
- Crée le commit via HEREDOC pour préserver le formatage multiligne.
- **Ne jamais** utiliser `--no-verify`, `--amend` (sauf demande explicite), ni `--no-gpg-sign`.
- Si un hook pre-commit échoue : diagnostique la cause, corrige, re-stage, et crée un **nouveau** commit (pas d'amend).

### 6. Vérification finale

Après commit, lance `git status` et `git log -1` pour confirmer. Reporte brièvement : sha court + sujet + (fixup le cas échéant).

## Garde-fous

- Ne push jamais.
- Ne crée pas de commit vide.
- Ne touche pas à `git config`.
- Si plusieurs "unités logiques" distinctes sont mélangées dans le diff, préviens l'utilisateur et propose de splitter plutôt que de tout entasser. Tu peux utiliser `git stash` (avec `-u`/`--keep-index` si pertinent) pour isoler des sous-ensembles — mais cette commande n'est **pas** auto-approuvée, l'utilisateur devra confirmer à chaque usage : ne l'utilise que si c'est vraiment nécessaire.

This session is being continued from a previous conversation that ran out of context. The summary below covers the earlier portion of the conversation.

Summary:
1. Primary Request and Intent:
   - Review all code review feedback/exchanges from PRs #3 (menubar usage metrics), #4 (usage details popover), and #5 (tech debt resolution)
   - Identify patterns of recurring errors across these PRs
   - Challenge and improve the current AI harness (rules, hooks, configurations) to prevent these errors from being repeated
   - User requested deterministic solutions first (gitdiff-watcher hooks), avoiding abstract approaches
   - Preference for "everything" — full implementation rather than partial
   - Explicit request to add a tech debt for fixing legacy SwiftLint warnings

2. Key Technical Concepts:
   - Swift concurrency patterns (cooperative thread pool, blocking, `queue.sync`, `queue.async`, `nonisolated(unsafe)`)
   - Error handling (try/try!/try?, associated values, error context propagation per `SWIFT-ERROR-HANDLING.md`)
   - Test robustness (`Task.sleep` with fixed literals vs. `eventually()` polling helpers)
   - NSFormatter thread safety and reuse patterns
   - Git-based CI/CD hooks via gitdiff-watcher
   - SwiftLint custom rules and baseline mechanism
   - SwiftPM build tool plugins (Swift 6)
   - Value objects for domain primitives
   - Entire.io for checkpoint/transcript analysis

3. Files and Code Sections:
   
   a) **Analysis & Documentation Files (Created/Modified):**
   - `.claude/scripts/check-swift-quality.sh` (created, later superseded)
     - 117-line bash script with 7 deterministic Swift quality checks
     - Pattern matching for E1 (try? on FS ops), E2 (flock LOCK_EX), W1-W5 rules
     - Calibrated against live codebase to ensure zero false positives
     - Ultimately removed when SwiftLint migration was completed
   
   - `.claude/rules/swift-quality.md` (modified)
     - Non-negotiable rules enumerated with references to `docs/SWIFT-*.md`
     - Updated to describe SwiftLint integration instead of bash script
     - Added command for baseline regeneration
   
   - `.claude/settings.json` (modified)
     - Added `check-swift-quality.sh` hook on Stop event (later removed when SwiftLint integrated into build)
     - Modified timeout from 120s to 180s on `swift build` to absorb SwiftLint plugin compilation
     - Replaced gitdiff-watcher quality check hook with direct build integration
   
   b) **SwiftLint Integration Files (Created):**
   - `AIUsagesTrackers/Package.swift` (modified)
     - Added dependency: `.package(url: "https://github.com/SimplyDanny/SwiftLintPlugins", from: "0.57.0")`
     - Attached `SwiftLintBuildToolPlugin` to all 3 targets: AIUsagesTrackersLib, AIUsagesTrackers (app), AIUsagesTrackersTests
     - Code snippet:
       ```swift
       dependencies: [
           .package(url: "https://github.com/SimplyDanny/SwiftLintPlugins", from: "0.57.0"),
       ],
       targets: [
           .target(
               name: "AIUsagesTrackersLib",
               path: "Sources/AIUsagesTrackers",
               plugins: [.plugin(name: "SwiftLintBuildToolPlugin", package: "SwiftLintPlugins")]
           ),
           // ... repeated for app and test targets
       ]
       ```
   
   - `AIUsagesTrackers/.swiftlint.yml` (created)
     - Custom rules E1, E2, W1, W3, W4, W5 (W2 excluded due to regex limitations)
     - `only_rules: [custom_rules]` to narrow surface to reviewed patterns only
     - `baseline: .swiftlint.baseline` to freeze 16 legacy violations
     - Example rule (E1):
       ```yaml
       e1_try_silencing_critical_fs:
           name: "try? on critical filesystem op"
           regex: 'try\?\s+(FileManager\.default\.)?(createDirectory|moveItem|copyItem|setAttributes)\b'
           message: "try? swallows failure on a correctness-critical FS op — use do/catch (SWIFT-ERROR-HANDLING §1)"
           severity: error
       ```
   
   - `AIUsagesTrackers/.swiftlint.baseline` (created)
     - Captures 16 pre-existing violations: 1×W1, 8×W3, 2×W4, 5×W5
     - Generated via `swift package plugin --allow-writing-to-package-directory swiftlint lint --write-baseline .swiftlint.baseline`
     - Suppresses legacy warnings while enforcing no new violations matching those rules
   
   c) **Tech Debt Documentation (Created):**
   - `tech-debt/REDACTED/debt.md` (created)
     - Documents 16 baseline violations with refactoring paths
     - W1: move ISO formatter off `nonisolated(unsafe) static let` to serial queue or per-call
     - W3: introduce `eventually()` helper and replace 8 fixed `Task.sleep` sites in tests
     - W4: add justification comments to 2 `@unchecked Sendable` instances
     - W5: lift 5 duration literals from comments into named constants
     - Criticality: medium, size: S

4. Errors and fixes:
   - **Issue: Bash script matched comment lines containing rule keywords**
     - Fixed by adding comment-line exclusion patterns: `^[[:space:]]*//` guards
     - Later: Problem became moot when migrated to SwiftLint (which has `excluded_match_kinds`)
   
   - **Issue: E2 regex flagged legitimate test code (flock in contention fixture)**
     - Fixed by adding `excluded: ["AIUsagesTrackers/Tests/.*\\.swift"]` to E2 rule
     - SwiftLint's `excluded` mechanism handles this cleanly
   
   - **Issue: W1/W4 SwiftLint rules fired on comment lines containing keywords**
     - Initial attempt with `match_kinds: [keyword, identifier]` was too restrictive, missed the actual code
     - Fixed by switching to `excluded_match_kinds: [comment, doccomment]` instead
     - This allows detection of the actual keyword while ignoring justification comments
   
   - **Issue: SwiftLint plugin invocation syntax**
     - User's question about "latest SwiftLint SwiftPM plugin invocation syntax (Swift 6)" led me to research
     - Confirmed command: `swift package plugin --allow-writing-to-package-directory swiftlint lint`
     - Correct URL from official recommendation: `https://github.com/SimplyDanny/SwiftLintPlugins` (not the main SwiftLint repo)
   
   - **Issue: First build with SwiftLint produced excessive warnings (trailing commas, force unwrap, etc.)**
     - Caused by using built-in rules instead of custom-only
     - Fixed by switching to `only_rules: [custom_rules]` in `.swiftlint.yml`
     - Result: Only 16 custom pattern violations remained (now baselined)

5. Problem Solving:
   - **Calibration of bash script:** Tested against full codebase to achieve 0 false positives initially. Adjusted for test-only contexts and comment exclusions.
   - **SwiftLint migration decision:** Recognized that SwiftLint provides superior AST-aware matching and built-in baseline mechanism vs. regex-based shell script. Trade-off: W2 (formatter allocation) cannot be expressed as a regex rule and would require AST-level analysis.
   - **Baseline strategy:** Froze 16 pre-existing violations to keep build green while enforcing no new violations. This requires explicit regeneration after fixes.
   - **Hook consolidation:** Removed dedicated quality-check hook and merged into existing `swift build` hook, reducing hook surface area and complexity.

6. All user messages:
   - "pourrais-tu revoir tous les échanges entire effectués pendant les commits de la PR #3, #4 et #5 et challenger le harness actuellement mis en place pour les challenger et identifier des axes d'améliorations pour éviter que les erreur remontées lors des reviews ne soient reiterees." [Extensive review and harness improvement request]
   - "il n'existe pas de linter swift qui permettrait d'implémenter tout ou partie des checks sans avoir à introduire un script sh dedié ?" [Question about SwiftLint vs custom bash]
   - "ok avec tout ca" [Confirmation to proceed with full SwiftLint migration]
   - "check latest SwiftLint SwiftPM plugin invocation syntax (Swift 6 package plugin command)" [Skill invocation request for config research]
   - "penses a rajouter une tech debt pour fixer les warnings legacy" [Reminder to add tech debt for baseline violations]

7. Pending Tasks:
   - **Tech debt resolution:** Fix the 16 legacy SwiftLint baseline violations (W1×1, W3×8, W4×2, W5×5) as documented in `tech-debt/REDACTED/debt.md`
   - **Optional:** Investigate AST-based approach for W2 rule (formatter allocation detection) if the pattern recurs in reviews
   - **Future:** Extend harness with semantic checks (sub-agent `swift-reviewer`) for rules requiring context beyond regex (e.g., error enum associated values)

8. Current Work:
   Immediately before the commit request, I had completed the full SwiftLint migration:
   - Created `AIUsagesTrackers/.swiftlint.yml` with 6 custom rules (E1, E2, W1, W3, W4, W5)
   - Generated `AIUsagesTrackers/.swiftlint.baseline` to freeze 16 legacy violations
   - Updated `AIUsagesTrackers/Package.swift` to add SwiftLintPlugins dependency and attach build tool plugin to all targets
   - Removed bash script `.claude/scripts/check-swift-quality.sh` and its associated hook
   - Updated `.claude/settings.json` to remove the dedicated quality-check hook and increase `swift build` timeout to 180s
   - Created tech-debt entry documenting the 16 baseline violations and refactoring paths
   - Updated `.claude/rules/swift-quality.md` to document SwiftLint integration and baseline regeneration process
   - Validated with `swift build` (0 warnings, 0 errors) and `swift package plugin swiftlint lint` (0 violations after baseline applied)

9. Optional Next Step:
   Commit all changes. The work is complete and cohesive:
   - SwiftLint is integrated into `swift build` for all three targets
   - Baseline suppresses legacy violations while enforcing no new violations
   - Tech debt documents explicit refactoring paths for the 16 violations
   - Rules documentation points users to the SwiftLint config and how to regenerate baseline
   
   The next immediate task after commit should be addressing the tech-debt items, starting with the lowest-hanging fruit (W5 — duration literal comments) which requires simple constant extraction, then W4 (add justification comments to `@unchecked Sendable`), then W3 (introduce and use `eventually()` helper), and finally W1 (refactor Logger formatter from `nonisolated(unsafe)` to safe pattern).

If you need specific details from before compaction (like exact code snippets, error messages, or content you generated), read the full transcript at: /Users/fcamblor/.claude/projects/-Users-fcamblor-Documents-projects-third-party-oss-mac-ai-trackers/4aff8f74-3024-4e55-b498-8cab98cb64a9.jsonl
Continue the conversation from where it left off without asking the user any further questions. Resume directly — do not acknowledge the summary, do not recap what was happening, do not preface with "I'll continue" or similar. Pick up the last task as if the break never happened.

file.content:
---
description: Swift production quality rules — load when writing or modifying Swift source files
globs: ["**/*.swift", "**/Package.swift"]
---

# Swift production quality

Before writing or modifying any Swift code, load and follow these docs:

- `docs/SWIFT-CONCURRENCY.md` — cooperative thread pool, NSFormatter thread safety, actors, Process timeouts
- `docs/SWIFT-ERROR-HANDLING.md` — no silent `try?`, no success-after-catch, rich error types
- `docs/SWIFT-IO-ROBUSTNESS.md` — atomic writes, flock with timeout, O(n+m) merges
- `docs/SWIFT-TESTABILITY.md` — dependency injection, test coverage, force-unwrap, comments, magic numbers
- `docs/SWIFT-VALUE-OBJECTS.md` — value objects for domain fields, struct vs enum, ExpressibleByXxx, Codable

## Non-negotiable rules

1. **Never block the cooperative thread pool** — no `Process.waitUntilExit()`, `readDataToEndOfFile()`, or `queue.sync` from an `async`/`actor` context without a background dispatch.

2. **Never use `try?` on operations whose failure affects correctness** — `createDirectory`, `write`, `flock`, file moves: propagate or handle explicitly with a log.

3. **Every external dependency must be injectable via a protocol** — `URLSession`, Keychain, `FileManager`, `Process`, clock. No direct calls to system APIs in business logic.

4. **Every public method must have at least one test** — especially network, I/O, lifecycle (start/stop idempotence), and all error paths.

5. **Always write files atomically** — use `Data.write(to:options: .atomic)` or write to a temp file then `replaceItemAt(_:withItemAt:)`. Never `removeItem` + `moveItem`.

6. **Error enums must carry associated values** with diagnostic context (status code, underlying error, path, etc.).

7. **Comment WHY, not WHAT** — no restating code in plain English. Extract magic numbers as named constants.

8. **Wrap domain primitives in value objects** — never use raw `String` or `Int` for fields with a distinct identity (emails, vendor names, ISO dates, percentages, durations). See `docs/SWIFT-VALUE-OBJECTS.md` for the pattern.

## Automated check via SwiftLint

`AIUsagesTrackers/.swiftlint.yml` declares six custom rules enforced by
SwiftLint, wired as a SwiftPM build tool plugin on every target. The rules
run on every `swift build` (and inside Xcode). Findings surface with these
codes — resolve errors before committing and avoid reintroducing warnings.

| Code | Severity | Pattern | Rule source |
|------|----------|---------|-------------|
| E1   | error    | `try?` on `createDirectory` / `moveItem` / `copyItem` / `setAttributes` | SWIFT-ERROR-HANDLING §1 |
| E2   | error    | `flock(..., LOCK_EX)` in production code without `LOCK_NB` | SWIFT-CONCURRENCY + SWIFT-IO-ROBUSTNESS |
| W1   | warning  | `nonisolated(unsafe)` in production sources | SWIFT-CONCURRENCY |
| W3   | warning  | `Task.sleep(literal)` in tests — prefer an `eventually()` poll-helper | SWIFT-TESTABILITY |
| W4   | warning  | `@unchecked Sendable` without an adjacent justification comment | SWIFT-CONCURRENCY |
| W5   | warning  | Comment embeds a bare `N unit` duration — drift risk after constant extraction | SWIFT-TESTABILITY §Comment WHY |

Violations already present when the rules were introduced are recorded in
`AIUsagesTrackers/.swiftlint.baseline` and do not fail the build; new
violations matching the same rules do. When a finding fires on a file you
did not author, fix it under the same change rather than leaving drift
behind, and regenerate the baseline with:

```
cd AIUsagesTrackers && swift package plugin --allow-writing-to-package-directory swiftlint lint --write-baseline .swiftlint.baseline
```

---
title: Resolve SwiftLint legacy warnings captured in .swiftlint.baseline
date: 2026-04-18
criticality: medium
size: S
---

## Problem

`AIUsagesTrackers/.swiftlint.baseline` lists 16 violations that existed when
the six custom rules (E1, E2, W1, W3, W4, W5) were introduced. Each
violation is a real finding already surfaced in prior review passes; the
baseline only silences them so the build stays green. As long as an entry
sits in the baseline, the rule is passively disabled for that site — a new
agent modifying the same region will not learn from the finding.

Breakdown:

- **W1 — `nonisolated(unsafe)` in production** (×1)
  - `Sources/AIUsagesTrackers/Logging/Logger.swift:31` — static ISO formatter.
- **W3 — `Task.sleep` literal in tests** (×8)
  - `Tests/AIUsagesTrackersTests/UsageStoreTests.swift:80, 369, 747`
  - `Tests/AIUsagesTrackersTests/UsagePollerTests.swift:143, 236`
  - `Tests/AIUsagesTrackersTests/UsagesFileWatcherTests.swift:33, 88, 114`
- **W4 — `@unchecked Sendable` without a justification comment** (×2)
  - `Tests/AIUsagesTrackersTests/ClaudeCodeConnectorTests.swift:7`
  - `Tests/AIUsagesTrackersTests/UsageStoreTests.swift:11`
- **W5 — duration literal embedded in a comment** (×5)
  - `Tests/AIUsagesTrackersTests/UsageStoreTests.swift:438 (×3), 510, 543`

## Impact

- **Maintainability**: baseline entries are a hidden rule carve-out; the
  longer they persist, the less the harness teaches new work.
- **AI code generation quality**: agents copying from these sites replicate
  the exact patterns the rules were introduced to prevent.
- **Bug/regression risk**: W1 (single thread formatter) and W3 (fragile
  async sync) are the real correctness/flake risks; W4 and W5 are code
  quality.

## Affected files / areas

- `AIUsagesTrackers/.swiftlint.baseline` — remove entries as each site is
  fixed.
- The 8 files listed above.

## Refactoring paths

1. **W1 in `Logger.swift`**: move `isoFormatter` off a `nonisolated(unsafe)
   static let` to a dedicated serial `DispatchQueue` or instantiate per
   call — `Logger` is not in a hot path, per-call allocation is acceptable.
2. **W3 in tests**: introduce an `eventually(timeout:interval:_:)` helper
   (already exists in `UsageStoreTests` — promote to a shared test utility)
   and replace every fixed `Task.sleep` that waits for an observable
   state change. Pure time-based waits (no observable state to poll, e.g.
   "no emit happens within N ms") can stay with a `Task.sleep` behind a
   named constant + rationale comment.
3. **W4**: add a one-line justification comment directly above each
   `@unchecked Sendable` conformance explaining why single-threaded access
   is safe.
4. **W5**: lift every `// N <unit>` comment to a named constant whose
   declaration site carries the rationale. Update the test body to reference
   the constant.
5. After each batch, regenerate `.swiftlint.baseline`:
   `swift package plugin --allow-writing-to-package-directory swiftlint lint --write-baseline .swiftlint.baseline`
   and commit the shrunken baseline alongside the fix.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] `.swiftlint.baseline` is empty or the file is removed along with the
  `baseline:` key in `.swiftlint.yml`.
- [ ] `swift build` passes with zero SwiftLint warnings.
- [ ] No new `Task.sleep(fixed literal)` remains on a site where an
  observable state could be polled instead.
- [ ] Every surviving `@unchecked Sendable` has a justification comment on
  the preceding line.

## Additional context

Introduced alongside the SwiftLint migration (see commit that adds
`AIUsagesTrackers/.swiftlint.yml`). The rules themselves correspond to
recurring findings from reviews on PRs #3–#5; see `.claude/rules/swift-quality.md`
for the rule catalog and `docs/SWIFT-*.md` for the underlying specs.

# SwiftLint configuration for AIUsagesTrackers.
#
# Rule codes mirror .claude/rules/swift-quality.md — keep the mapping in sync.
# Severities: `error` fails the build; `warning` surfaces in logs / IDE.

included:
  - Sources
  - Tests

# Violations already present when these rules were introduced are stored in
# .swiftlint.baseline. Each legacy warning should be resolved individually and
# removed from the baseline; new violations matching the same rules still fail
# the build.
baseline: .swiftlint.baseline

# Narrow the SwiftLint surface to exactly the codes documented in
# .claude/rules/swift-quality.md. Built-in rules are deliberately NOT enabled —
# we want a low-noise, high-signal lint focused on the patterns that reviews
# keep flagging. Reach for built-in rules only if a review finding recurs that
# a custom rule cannot express precisely.
only_rules:
  - custom_rules

custom_rules:
  e1_try_silencing_critical_fs:
    name: "try? on critical filesystem op"
    regex: 'try\?\s+(FileManager\.default\.)?(createDirectory|moveItem|copyItem|setAttributes)\b'
    message: "try? swallows failure on a correctness-critical FS op — use do/catch (SWIFT-ERROR-HANDLING §1)"
    severity: error

  e2_flock_lock_ex_without_nb:
    name: "flock LOCK_EX without LOCK_NB"
    regex: 'flock\([^)]*LOCK_EX(?![^)]*LOCK_NB)'
    message: "flock with LOCK_EX must be OR'd with LOCK_NB and wrapped in a timeout loop (SWIFT-CONCURRENCY / SWIFT-IO-ROBUSTNESS)"
    severity: error
    excluded:
      - "AIUsagesTrackers/Tests/.*\\.swift"

  w1_nonisolated_unsafe:
    name: "nonisolated(unsafe) in production"
    regex: 'nonisolated\(unsafe\)'
    excluded_match_kinds:
      - comment
      - doccomment
    message: "nonisolated(unsafe) bypasses Swift isolation — prefer actor-isolated storage or per-call allocation (SWIFT-CONCURRENCY)"
    severity: warning
    excluded:
      - "AIUsagesTrackers/Tests/.*\\.swift"

  w3_task_sleep_literal_in_tests:
    name: "Task.sleep with fixed literal in tests"
    regex: 'Task\.sleep\([^)]*[0-9]'
    message: "Task.sleep with a fixed literal is fragile under CI load — poll the observable state with the eventually() helper (SWIFT-TESTABILITY)"
    severity: warning
    included:
      - "AIUsagesTrackers/Tests/.*\\.swift"

  w4_unchecked_sendable:
    name: "@unchecked Sendable"
    regex: '@unchecked\s+Sendable'
    excluded_match_kinds:
      - comment
      - doccomment
    message: "@unchecked Sendable requires an adjacent justification comment describing the single-threaded contract (SWIFT-CONCURRENCY)"
    severity: warning

  w5_duration_literal_in_comment:
    name: "Duration literal embedded in comment"
    regex: '\b\d+\s*(hours?|days?|minutes?|seconds?|ms)\b'
    match_kinds:
      - comment
    message: "Comment embeds a bare `N unit` duration — lift to a named constant whose declaration site carries the rationale (SWIFT-TESTABILITY §Comment WHY)"
    severity: warning

// swift-tools-version:6.0
import PackageDescription

let package = Package(
    name: "AIUsagesTrackers",
    platforms: [.macOS(.v14)],
    dependencies: [
        .package(url: "https://github.com/SimplyDanny/SwiftLintPlugins", from: "0.57.0"),
    ],
    targets: [
        .target(
            name: "AIUsagesTrackersLib",
            path: "Sources/AIUsagesTrackers",
            plugins: [.plugin(name: "SwiftLintBuildToolPlugin", package: "SwiftLintPlugins")]
        ),
        .executableTarget(
            name: "AIUsagesTrackers",
            dependencies: ["AIUsagesTrackersLib"],
            path: "Sources/App",
            plugins: [.plugin(name: "SwiftLintBuildToolPlugin", package: "SwiftLintPlugins")]
        ),
        .testTarget(
            name: "AIUsagesTrackersTests",
            dependencies: ["AIUsagesTrackersLib"],
            path: "Tests/AIUsagesTrackersTests",
            plugins: [.plugin(name: "SwiftLintBuildToolPlugin", package: "SwiftLintPlugins")]
        ),
    ]
)

skills.name: commit
skills.content:
# Smart commit

Tu dois committer les changements git en cours dans le repo courant en suivant les guidelines ci-dessous.

## Contexte utilisateur

Intent fourni par l'utilisateur (peut être vide) : ``

## Étapes

### 1. Analyser l'état du repo

Lance en parallèle :
- `git status` (sans `-uall`)
- `git diff --staged` et `git diff` pour voir les changements
- `git log -n 5 --pretty=format:'%h %s%n%b%n---'` pour voir les 4-5 derniers commits

### 2. Détecter les conventions du projet

À partir des 4-5 derniers commits :
- **Conventional commits ?** Si la majorité suit le format `type(scope): message` (feat, fix, chore, docs, refactor, test, perf, build, ci...) → utilise ce format. Sinon → format libre.
- **Langue ?** Détecte la langue dominante des messages (français, anglais, autre) → utilise la même langue. En cas de doute, français.
- **Style ?** Impératif vs descriptif, majuscule initiale ou non, présence de scope, etc. → imite.

### 3. Détecter un éventuel fixup

Si l'intent utilisateur mentionne un correctif (mots-clés : "fix", "corrige", "répare", "bug", "issue", "problème", "régression", "broken"...) :

1. Identifie les fichiers modifiés (`git diff --name-only HEAD` + fichiers staged).
2. Pour chaque fichier, regarde `git log -n 10 --oneline -- <fichier>` pour voir quels commits récents l'ont touché.
3. Si **un seul commit récent** (dans les ~5 derniers, non encore pushé sur main/master partagé — vérifie avec `git log @{u}..HEAD` si upstream) est clairement à l'origine du code cassé, propose un **`git commit --fixup=<sha>`** au lieu d'un commit classique.
4. Si ambigu ou si le commit date d'avant `@{u}` (déjà pushé en amont partagé), reste sur un commit classique.

Ne fais **pas** de rebase autosquash automatiquement — laisse ce soin à l'utilisateur.

### 4. Rédiger le message

Règles impératives :
- **Le message explique le POURQUOI**, pas le QUOI. Le diff dit déjà ce qui change. Le message dit pourquoi ce changement existe (motivation, contrainte, problème résolu, contexte métier).
- Sujet court (≤72 caractères), en suivant la convention détectée.
- Si le pourquoi ne tient pas dans le sujet, ajoute un corps (ligne vide + explication). Sinon, pas de corps inutile.
- Pas de name-dropping de fichiers/fonctions dans le sujet — le diff suffit.
- Pas de phrases vides genre « Mise à jour du code » ou « Divers fixes ».

Si l'intent utilisateur est vide ou trop vague pour déduire le pourquoi, et que le diff ne le rend pas évident, **demande à l'utilisateur** avant de committer plutôt que d'inventer.

### 5. Staging et commit

- Si rien n'est staged, stage les fichiers pertinents **par nom** (jamais `git add -A` ni `git add .` pour éviter d'embarquer secrets/binaires).
- Ignore les fichiers suspects (`.env`, `credentials*`, clés privées, dumps volumineux). Préviens l'utilisateur si tu en vois.
- Crée le commit via HEREDOC pour préserver le formatage multiligne.
- **Ne jamais** utiliser `--no-verify`, `--amend` (sauf demande explicite), ni `--no-gpg-sign`.
- Si un hook pre-commit échoue : diagnostique la cause, corrige, re-stage, et crée un **nouveau** commit (pas d'amend).

### 6. Vérification finale

Après commit, lance `git status` et `git log -1` pour confirmer. Reporte brièvement : sha court + sujet + (fixup le cas échéant).

## Garde-fous

- Ne push jamais.
- Ne crée pas de commit vide.
- Ne touche pas à `git config`.
- Si plusieurs "unités logiques" distinctes sont mélangées dans le diff, préviens l'utilisateur et propose de splitter plutôt que de tout entasser. Tu peux utiliser `git stash` (avec `-u`/`--keep-index` si pertinent) pour isoler des sous-ensembles — mais cette commande n'est **pas** auto-approuvée, l'utilisateur devra confirmer à chaque usage : ne l'utilise que si c'est vraiment nécessaire.

## context7
Use this server to fetch current documentation whenever the user asks about a library, framework, SDK, API, CLI tool, or cloud service -- even well-known ones like React, Next.js, Prisma, Express, Tailwind, Django, or Spring Boot. This includes API syntax, configuration, version migration, library-specific debugging, setup instructions, and CLI tool usage. Use even when you think you know the answer -- your training data may not reflect recent changes. Prefer this over web search for library docs.

Do not use for: refactoring, writing scripts from scratch, debugging business logic, code review, or general programming concepts.

## plugin:serena:serena
You have access to semantic coding tools upon which you rely heavily for all your work.
You operate in a resource-efficient and intelligent manner, always keeping in mind to not read or generate
content that is not needed for the task at hand.

Some tasks may require you to understand the architecture of large parts of the codebase, while for others,
it may be enough to read a small set of symbols or a single file.
You avoid reading entire files unless it is absolutely necessary, instead relying on intelligent step-by-step 
acquisition of information. Once you have read a full file, it does not make
sense to analyse it with the symbolic read tools; you already have the information.

You can achieve intelligent reading of code by using the symbolic tools for getting an overview of symbols and
the relations between them, and then only reading the bodies of symbols that are necessary to complete the task at hand. 
You can use the standard tools like list_dir, find_file and search_for_pattern if you need to.
Where appropriate, you pass the `relative_path` parameter to restrict the search to a specific file or directory.

If you are unsure about a symbol's name or location (to the extent that substring_matching for the symbol name is not enough), you can use the `search_for_pattern` tool, which allows fast
and flexible search for patterns in the codebase. In this way, you can first find candidates for symbols or files,
and then proceed with the symbolic tools.



Symbols are identified by their `name_path` and `relative_path` (see the description of the `find_symbol` tool).
You can get information about the symbols in a file by using the `get_symbols_overview` tool or use the `find_symbol` to search. 
You only read the bodies of symbols when you need to (e.g. if you want to fully understand or edit it).
For example, if you are working with Python code and already know that you need to read the body of the constructor of the class Foo, you can directly
use `find_symbol` with name path pattern `Foo/__init__` and `includ… [truncated]

## vibe_kanban
A Vibe Kanban MCP server for task, issue, repository, workspace, and session management. Use list/read tools first when you need IDs or current state. TOOLS: 'add_issue_tag', 'assign_issue', 'create_issue', 'create_issue_relationship', 'create_session', 'delete_issue', 'delete_issue_relationship', 'delete_workspace', 'get_execution', 'get_issue', 'get_repo', 'link_workspace_issue', 'list_issue_assignees', 'list_issue_priorities', 'list_issue_tags', 'list_issues', 'list_org_members', 'list_organizations', 'list_projects', 'list_repos', 'list_sessions', 'list_tags', 'list_workspaces', 'remove_issue_tag', 'run_session_prompt', 'start_workspace', 'unassign_issue', 'update_cleanup_script', 'update_dev_server_script', 'update_issue', 'update_session', 'update_setup_script', 'update_workspace'.

content.content:
# Markdown authoring rules

## Language: English only

All Markdown files in this repository MUST be written in English, regardless of the conversation language. This includes `CLAUDE.md`, every file under `docs/`, `README.md`, and any new `*.md`.

- When creating a new Markdown file, write it in English from the start even if the user's prompt is in another language.
- When editing an existing Markdown file that contains non-English prose, translate the edited sections to English instead of preserving mixed languages.
- Code blocks, file paths, commands, and identifiers keep their original form — this rule applies to natural-language prose only.

## Write for longevity

Markdown in this repository is long-lived context. Optimize each file so it does not need to be rewritten as the code evolves. Avoid anything that ties documentation to a specific moment in time or to renamable artifacts:

- Do NOT reference specific commit SHAs, PR numbers, or branch names.
- Do NOT describe the project as being in a transient state ("currently a Hello World", "not yet implemented", "WIP"). If a feature does not exist, simply do not document it.
- Do NOT speculate about intent or future direction ("the name suggests…", "will eventually…"). Document what the code actually does today in terms that remain true tomorrow.
- Do NOT list every file, symbol, or dependency when a structural description is enough — exhaustive lists drift out of date quickly.
- Avoid naming specific files, types, or symbols that could be renamed or moved. Prefer describing roles and responsibilities ("the SwiftUI entry point", "the package manifest") over fixed identifiers. When a path is truly stable because a tool mandates it (e.g. `Package.swift` for Swift Package Manager, `.claude/rules/` for Claude Code), it is fine to use it.
- Do NOT duplicate information that already lives authoritatively in code or tool manifests (version numbers, platform minimums, target lists). Point readers at the source of truth instead of mirroring it.

When in doubt, ask: "Will this sentence still be accurate after the next three features ship, or after a plausible rename?" If not, rephrase or remove it.

## Single source of truth across Markdown files

When two Markdown files cover the same topic, exactly one is the authoritative spec; the other references it and adds only what is genuinely its own (workflow steps, scaffolding procedure, agent phases, etc.).

This applies in particular to these pairs:

- `docs/<topic>.md` (spec) ↔ `.claude/skills/<name>/SKILL.md` (skill that implements it)
- `docs/<topic>.md` (spec) ↔ `.claude/agents/<name>.md` (agent prompt that uses it)
- `docs/<topic>.md` (spec) ↔ `.claude/commands/<name>.md` (slash command that triggers it)

Rules:

- Templates, file formats, naming conventions, and directory layouts belong in the spec, NOT in the operational artifact.
- The operational artifact MUST start with a one-line pointer ("Read `docs/<topic>.md` first; it defines X, Y, Z.") and MUST include a conflict-resolution line ("If this artifact disagrees with `docs/<topic>.md`, the spec wins.").
- Before adding concrete content (a template block, a format string, a list of sections) to an operational artifact, check whether that content already exists in a `docs/` spec. If it does, reference it instead of copying it.

## Exception: `roadmap/`

Files under `roadmap/` intentionally describe planned, not-yet-implemented work. The "Write for longevity" rules above (no transient state, no speculation about future direction) do NOT apply there — a roadmap is precisely a record of intent and ordering that will evolve as features ship. The English-only rule still applies. See `docs/ROADMAP.md` for the roadmap file conventions.

content.text:
# Smart commit

Tu dois committer les changements git en cours dans le repo courant en suivant les guidelines ci-dessous.

## Contexte utilisateur

Intent fourni par l'utilisateur (peut être vide) : ``

## Étapes

### 1. Analyser l'état du repo

Lance en parallèle :
- `git status` (sans `-uall`)
- `git diff --staged` et `git diff` pour voir les changements
- `git log -n 5 --pretty=format:'%h %s%n%b%n---'` pour voir les 4-5 derniers commits

### 2. Détecter les conventions du projet

À partir des 4-5 derniers commits :
- **Conventional commits ?** Si la majorité suit le format `type(scope): message` (feat, fix, chore, docs, refactor, test, perf, build, ci...) → utilise ce format. Sinon → format libre.
- **Langue ?** Détecte la langue dominante des messages (français, anglais, autre) → utilise la même langue. En cas de doute, français.
- **Style ?** Impératif vs descriptif, majuscule initiale ou non, présence de scope, etc. → imite.

### 3. Détecter un éventuel fixup

Si l'intent utilisateur mentionne un correctif (mots-clés : "fix", "corrige", "répare", "bug", "issue", "problème", "régression", "broken"...) :

1. Identifie les fichiers modifiés (`git diff --name-only HEAD` + fichiers staged).
2. Pour chaque fichier, regarde `git log -n 10 --oneline -- <fichier>` pour voir quels commits récents l'ont touché.
3. Si **un seul commit récent** (dans les ~5 derniers, non encore pushé sur main/master partagé — vérifie avec `git log @{u}..HEAD` si upstream) est clairement à l'origine du code cassé, propose un **`git commit --fixup=<sha>`** au lieu d'un commit classique.
4. Si ambigu ou si le commit date d'avant `@{u}` (déjà pushé en amont partagé), reste sur un commit classique.

Ne fais **pas** de rebase autosquash automatiquement — laisse ce soin à l'utilisateur.

### 4. Rédiger le message

Règles impératives :
- **Le message explique le POURQUOI**, pas le QUOI. Le diff dit déjà ce qui change. Le message dit pourquoi ce changement existe (motivation, contrainte, problème résolu, contexte métier).
- Sujet court (≤72 caractères), en suivant la convention détectée.
- Si le pourquoi ne tient pas dans le sujet, ajoute un corps (ligne vide + explication). Sinon, pas de corps inutile.
- Pas de name-dropping de fichiers/fonctions dans le sujet — le diff suffit.
- Pas de phrases vides genre « Mise à jour du code » ou « Divers fixes ».

Si l'intent utilisateur est vide ou trop vague pour déduire le pourquoi, et que le diff ne le rend pas évident, **demande à l'utilisateur** avant de committer plutôt que d'inventer.

### 5. Staging et commit

- Si rien n'est staged, stage les fichiers pertinents **par nom** (jamais `git add -A` ni `git add .` pour éviter d'embarquer secrets/binaires).
- Ignore les fichiers suspects (`.env`, `credentials*`, clés privées, dumps volumineux). Préviens l'utilisateur si tu en vois.
- Crée le commit via HEREDOC pour préserver le formatage multiligne.
- **Ne jamais** utiliser `--no-verify`, `--amend` (sauf demande explicite), ni `--no-gpg-sign`.
- Si un hook pre-commit échoue : diagnostique la cause, corrige, re-stage, et crée un **nouveau** commit (pas d'amend).

### 6. Vérification finale

Après commit, lance `git status` et `git log -1` pour confirmer. Reporte brièvement : sha court + sujet + (fixup le cas échéant).

## Garde-fous

- Ne push jamais.
- Ne crée pas de commit vide.
- Ne touche pas à `git config`.
- Si plusieurs "unités logiques" distinctes sont mélangées dans le diff, préviens l'utilisateur et propose de splitter plutôt que de tout entasser. Tu peux utiliser `git stash` (avec `-u`/`--keep-index` si pertinent) pour isoler des sous-ensembles — mais cette commande n'est **pas** auto-approuvée, l'utilisateur devra confirmer à chaque usage : ne l'utilise que si c'est vraiment nécessaire.

content.content:
d23a934 chore(settings): permit entire CLI commands and docs access
Enable execution of entire CLI for task/todo/session hooks and allow
fetching documentation from docs.entire.io. This integrates the entire
workflow system for better task tracking and lifecycle management.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>
Entire-Checkpoint: b2de96f9a52a

---
853683f feat(store): exclude per-model metrics from menu bar display
Show only top-level aggregate metrics (session, weekly) in the compact
menu bar label. Per-model breakdowns (weekly_sonnet, weekly_opus) are
displayed only in the popover, which has more space.

Add test coverage for the exclusion filter.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>
Entire-Checkpoint: 8f151c9c4fb8

---
94ea475 chore(roadmap): close claude-model-weekly-metrics epic
All acceptance criteria pass. Mark epic done and remove plan file.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>
Entire-Checkpoint: bf7a64a31c86

---
f1164ec fix(connector): restore Weekly Sonnet/Weekly Opus label casing per epic spec
Review recommendation #10 harmonized names to snake_case, but the epic
acceptance criteria explicitly require title-case "Weekly Sonnet"/"Weekly Opus".
Reverts the name change to satisfy the contract.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>
Entire-Checkpoint: e98bc110145b

---
712fcd8 fix(tests): merge per-model suite into serialized fetch suite and drain logger
Separate serialized suites sharing MockURLProtocol.handler race against
each other. Merging into the existing suite ensures sequential execution.
Debug log test now calls waitForPendingWrites() because the logger
writes asynchronously on a serial dispatch queue.

Entire-Checkpoint: 809c7ba08792

---

.claude/rules/swift-quality.md --- Text
30 30 7. **Comment WHY, not WHAT** — no restating code in plain English. Extract magic numbers as named constants.
31 31 
32 32 8. **Wrap domain primitives in value objects** — never use raw `String` or `Int` for fields with a distinct identity (emails, vendor names, ISO dates, percentages, durations). See `docs/SWIFT-VALUE-OBJECTS.md` for the pattern.
   33 
   34 ## Automated check via SwiftLint
   35 
   36 `AIUsagesTrackers/.swiftlint.yml` declares six custom rules enforced by
   37 SwiftLint, wired as a SwiftPM build tool plugin on every target. The rules
   38 run on every `swift build` (and inside Xcode). Findings surface with these
   39 codes — resolve errors before committing and avoid reintroducing warnings.
   40 
   41 | Code | Severity | Pattern | Rule source |
   42 |------|----------|---------|-------------|
   43 | E1   | error    | `try?` on `createDirectory` / `moveItem` / `copyItem` / `setAttributes` | SWIFT-ERROR-HANDLING §1 |
   44 | E2   | error    | `flock(..., LOCK_EX)` in production code without `LOCK_NB` | SWIFT-CONCURRENCY + SWIFT-IO-ROBUSTNESS |
   45 | W1   | warning  | `nonisolated(unsafe)` in production sources | SWIFT-CONCURRENCY |
   46 | W3   | warning  | `Task.sleep(literal)` in tests — prefer an `eventually()` poll-helper | SWIFT-TESTABILITY |
   47 | W4   | warning  | `@unchecked Sendable` without an adjacent justification comment | SWIFT-CONCURRENCY |
   48 | W5   | warning  | Comment embeds a bare `N unit` duration — drift risk after constant extraction | SWIFT-TESTABILITY §Comment WHY |
   49 
   50 Violations already present when the rules were introduced are recorded in
   51 `AIUsagesTrackers/.swiftlint.baseline` and do not fail the build; new

30 30 7. **Comment WHY, not WHAT** — no restating code in plain English. Extract magic numbers as named constants.
31 31 
32 32 8. **Wrap domain primitives in value objects** — never use raw `String` or `Int` for fields with a distinct identity (emails, vendor names, ISO dates, percentages, durations). See `docs/SWIFT-VALUE-OBJECTS.md` for the pattern.
   33 
   34 ## Automated check via SwiftLint
   35 
   36 `AIUsagesTrackers/.swiftlint.yml` declares six custom rules enforced by
   37 SwiftLint, wired as a SwiftPM build tool plugin on every target. The rules
   38 run on every `swift build` (and inside Xcode). Findings surface with these
   39 codes — resolve errors before committing and avoid reintroducing warnings.
   40 
   41 | Code | Severity | Pattern | Rule source |
   42 |------|----------|---------|-------------|
   43 | E1   | error    | `try?` on `createDirectory` / `moveItem` / `copyItem` / `setAttributes` | SWIFT-ERROR-HANDLING §1 |
   44 | E2   | error    | `flock(..., LOCK_EX)` in production code without `LOCK_NB` | SWIFT-CONCURRENCY + SWIFT-IO-ROBUSTNESS |
   45 | W1   | warning  | `nonisolated(unsafe)` in production sources | SWIFT-CONCURRENCY |
   46 | W3   | warning  | `Task.sleep(literal)` in tests — prefer an `eventually()` poll-helper | SWIFT-TESTABILITY |
   47 | W4   | warning  | `@unchecked Sendable` without an adjacent justification comment | SWIFT-CONCURRENCY |
   48 | W5   | warning  | Comment embeds a bare `N unit` duration — drift risk after constant extraction | SWIFT-TESTABILITY §Comment WHY |
   49 
   50 Violations already present when the rules were introduced are recorded in
   51 `AIUsagesTrackers/.swiftlint.baseline` and do not fail the build; new
   52 violations matching the same rules do. When a finding fires on a file you
   53 did not author, fix it under the same change rather than leaving drift
   54 behind, and regenerate the baseline with:

   58 ```

input.command:
git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
feat(linting): integrate SwiftLint with custom rules and baseline

Enforce Swift quality patterns detected in reviews of PRs #3–#5 using
six custom SwiftLint rules (E1, E2, W1, W3, W4, W5). Rules target:

- E1: try? swallowing failures on critical filesystem ops
- E2: flock(LOCK_EX) without timeout safeguard (LOCK_NB)
- W1: nonisolated(unsafe) in production code
- W3: Task.sleep with fixed literal in tests (prefer eventually)
- W4: @unchecked Sendable without justification comment
- W5: duration literal embedded in comment (extract to constant)

Add SwiftLintPlugins dependency to Package.swift and attach build tool
plugin to all three targets (lib, app, tests). Rules run on every
`swift build` and inside Xcode.

Baseline 16 pre-existing violations (1×W1, 8×W3, 2×W4, 5×W5) to keep
build green while enforcing zero new violations. Regenerate baseline
after fixing violations via:

  cd AIUsagesTrackers && swift package plugin --allow-writing-to-package-directory swiftlint lint --write-baseline .swiftlint.baseline

Add tech-debt entry documenting refactoring paths for each violation.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)"

SwiftLint integration now enforces six custom quality rules (E1, E2, W1, W3, W4, W5) detected across prior PRs. 16 pre-existing violations baselined; new violations will fail the build. Next: resolve the legacy violations documented in tech-debt.
